steps.title: Spot the category-and-examples pattern
steps.content: The sentence ends: "often references at least one of several ______ movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture." The word "several" names a general category (themes), and "movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" are the specific examples listing what those themes are. A colon introduces a list that elaborates on the preceding noun.

steps.title: Apply the colon rule
steps.content:
Rule: use a colon after a complete clause to introduce a list or elaboration. Test each reconstructed sentence:

A) themes; movement, → "several themes; movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" — a semicolon joins two independent clauses, but "movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" is not a clause. Fails.

B) themes. Movement, → "several themes. Movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" — a period creates two sentences; the second becomes a fragment with no verb. Fails.

C) themes movement, → "several themes movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" — no punctuation leaves the noun "themes" and the list fused without any signal that the list is elaborating on "themes." Fails.

D) themes: movement, → "several themes: movement, the environment, and Kalabari culture" — the colon correctly introduces the list of specific themes. Correct.
